senses.definition: Chemical compounds that are derivatives of resorcinol, used mainly in making dyes, medicines, and special resins.
examples: These polymers are made from resorcinols and formaldehyde in controlled conditions.
Some resorcinols are studied for their potential medical applications.

senses.definition: An older or less common plural form of resorcinol, meaning chemical compounds based on resorcinol used in industry and medicine.
examples: Historically, different resorcins were isolated from natural resins.
The paper compares several resorcins and their reactivity.

senses.definition: A Latin-derived name for resorcinol, used especially in older medical or pharmaceutical texts.
examples: The ointment contains resorcinum and salicylic acid.
In older formularies, resorcinol is listed as resorcinum.

senses.definition: Relating to or derived from resorcinol, especially in the names of certain organic acids.
examples: They synthesized a new resorcylic acid lactone for testing.
Resorcylic derivatives are important in medicinal chemistry.

senses.definition: The process of a substance being absorbed back into a body or system, especially in biological tissues.
examples: The X-ray showed significant bone resorption around the tooth.
Calcium resorption increases when vitamin D levels are low.

senses.definition: Plural of resorption: several cases or types of a substance being absorbed back into a body or system.
examples: The study examined different bone resorptions in postmenopausal women.
Abnormal root resorptions can lead to tooth loss.

senses.definition: Causing or involving resorption, especially of bone or other tissues.
examples: Osteoclasts have a strong resorptive effect on bone.
The drug reduces resorptive activity in the skeleton.

senses.definition: A place where many people go on holiday to relax or enjoy special activities.
examples: They stayed at a beach resort for a week.
The town has grown into a popular ski resort.

senses.definition: The action of using something because there is no other good option.
examples: As a last resort, they contacted the police.
Strikes should be a final resort, not the first step.

senses.definition: To use something, especially an unpleasant method, because there is no other choice.
examples: They had to resort to borrowing money from friends.
He sometimes resorts to threats to get what he wants.

senses.definition: Past tense of resort: used something because there was no other good option.
examples: They finally resorted to legal action.
The company resorted to cutting jobs to survive.

senses.definition: Someone who resorts to something, especially a certain method or place, usually in a repeated or characteristic way.
examples: He is a frequent resorter to legal threats.
The study described the typical resorter to such tactics.

senses.definition: People who resort to something, especially a method or place, often in a repeated or characteristic way.
examples: The report warns about resorters to online fraud.
Habitual resorters to violence may face harsher penalties.

senses.definition: The act of using something, especially an unpleasant method, because there seems to be no other option.
examples: They are resorting to price cuts to stay competitive.
Instead of resorting to lies, he told the truth.

senses.definition: Plural of resort: several places where people go on holiday to relax or enjoy activities.
examples: The coastline is lined with beach resorts.
Ski resorts are busy during the winter months.

senses.definition: Third person singular of resort: uses something because there is no other good option.
examples: He often resorts to sarcasm when he is angry.
The government resorts to borrowing in times of crisis.

senses.definition: A bright red, fluorescent chemical compound used as an indicator in biochemical tests and cell studies.
examples: Resorufin is produced from resazurin in many cell viability assays.
The fluorescence of resorufin increases as the reaction proceeds.

senses.definition: An old or rare past tense and past participle of seek, meaning looked for again.
examples: He resought the missing documents in every drawer.
The answer was resought in later experiments.

senses.definition: To be filled with a loud sound that continues for some time.
examples: The hall resounded with applause.
The forest resounded with birdsong at dawn.

senses.definition: To be talked about or mentioned a lot, especially in praise or criticism.
examples: Her name resounded in the news after the award.
The scandal resounded throughout the country.

senses.definition: Past tense of resound: was filled with a loud, continuing sound.
examples: The stadium resounded with cheering fans.
The cave resounded as the waves crashed inside.

senses.definition: A person or thing that resounds or causes something to resound, especially in a figurative sense.
examples: The speech proved to be a powerful resounder of public opinion.
The bell is the regular resounder of the hour.

senses.definition: Very loud and clear, continuing for some time.
examples: There was a resounding crash as the tree fell.
The speech ended with resounding applause.

senses.definition: In a very strong, clear, and impressive way.
examples: The plan failed resoundingly.
The proposal was resoundingly approved by the board.

senses.definition: Third person singular of resound: is filled with a loud sound that continues for some time.
examples: The stadium resounds with cheering every weekend.
The hall resounds with music during the festival.

senses.definition: Something such as money, materials, or skills that a person or organization has and can use.
examples: We do not have enough resources to finish the project.
The school must use its resources more efficiently.

senses.definition: Something that provides help or information.
examples: The website is a useful resource for language learners.
Your teacher is an important resource when you have questions.

senses.definition: Having the money, people, or materials needed to do something.
examples: The project is well resourced and likely to succeed.
Smaller schools are often poorly resourced.

senses.definition: Past tense of resource: provided with money, people, or materials needed for a task.
examples: The program was resourced through government grants.
The team resourced the campaign carefully.

senses.definition: Good at finding quick and clever ways to solve problems.
examples: She is very resourceful and can handle any difficulty.
A resourceful teacher can make lessons work with few materials.

senses.definition: In a way that shows you can find quick and clever solutions to problems.
examples: They resourcefully reused old materials to build the shelter.
She handled the crisis resourcefully and calmly.

senses.definition: The ability to deal with difficult situations by finding quick and clever solutions.
examples: His resourcefulness impressed his colleagues.
Surviving in the wilderness requires great resourcefulness.

senses.definition: Rare plural of resourcefulness, referring to different kinds or examples of the ability to solve problems creatively.
examples: The book celebrates the many resourcefulnesses of the human spirit.
Different cultures show their resourcefulnesses in unique ways.

senses.definition: Having no money, support, or ability to solve problems.
examples: The refugees felt completely resourceless in the new country.
Without friends or savings, he was resourceless and alone.

senses.definition: The state of having no money, support, or ability to deal with problems.
examples: Her sudden resourcelessness after losing her job was frightening.
The novel explores the resourcelessness of the poor.

senses.definition: Plural of resource: all the money, materials, skills, and people that an organization or person has and can use.
examples: The country has rich natural resources.
We must use our resources more wisely.

senses.definition: The process of providing money, people, or materials needed for a project or organization.
examples: Effective resourcing is essential for long-term success.
The report discusses the resourcing of public services.

senses.definition: Present participle of resource: providing something with the money, people, or materials it needs.
examples: They are resourcing the new program through external grants.
The department is resourcing several community projects.

senses.definition: A rare or likely incorrect form of resolutive, meaning able to dissolve or bring something to an end.
examples: The term 'resoutive' appears only in a few old legal texts.
Some authors seem to use 'resoutive' where 'resolutive' is intended.

senses.definition: To sow seeds again in the same area.
examples: Farmers often resow the field after heavy rain washes away the seeds.
You may need to resow patches of grass in spring.

senses.definition: Past tense of resow: sowed seeds again in the same area.
examples: They resowed the damaged field in early May.
The gardener resowed the empty flowerbeds.

senses.definition: The act of sowing seeds again in the same area.
examples: Resowing is necessary after a hard winter.
The farmer planned the resowing of the pasture.

senses.definition: Present participle of resow: sowing seeds again in the same area.
examples: They are resowing the field after the flood.
She spent the afternoon resowing bare patches in the lawn.

senses.definition: Past participle of resow: having had seeds sown again.
examples: The field has been resown with a hardier crop.
Recently resown areas should be watered daily.

senses.definition: Third person singular of resow: sows seeds again in the same area.
examples: Each year she resows the wildflower garden.
The farmer resows after checking the soil.

senses.definition: Written short form of respectively, used in lists to show which items match each other in order.
examples: The heights of the two peaks are 800 m and 950 m, resp.
Samples A and B were stored at 4°C and 25°C, resp.

senses.definition: In some contexts, short form for respiratory or respiration.
examples: The patient’s resp rate was monitored hourly.
The report notes several abnormalities in resp function.

senses.definition: To change the spaces between letters, words, or lines in a piece of text or design.
examples: You may need to respace the text to fit the page.
The designer respaced the logo to improve readability.

senses.definition: Past tense of respace: changed the spaces between letters, words, or lines.
examples: She respaced the paragraphs to make the document shorter.
The title was respaced to center it on the page.

senses.definition: Third person singular of respace: changes the spaces between letters, words, or lines.
examples: The software automatically respaces the text.
He respaces the title whenever the font size changes.

senses.definition: The act of changing the spaces between letters, words, or lines in text or design.
examples: Respacing of the text improved the document’s appearance.
The project involved a complete respacing of the logo.

senses.definition: Present participle of respace: changing the spaces between letters, words, or lines.
examples: She is respacing the paragraphs to save space.
Respacing the logo can make it easier to read.

senses.definition: To dig or turn over soil again with a spade.
examples: You should respade the garden before planting new seeds.
The farmer respaded the soil to improve its structure.

senses.definition: Past tense of respade: dug or turned over soil again with a spade.
examples: He respaded the vegetable patch in early spring.
The soil was respaded to break up hard clumps.

senses.definition: Third person singular of respade: digs or turns over soil again with a spade.
examples: Each year he respades the flowerbeds.
The gardener respades the soil before planting.

senses.definition: The act of digging or turning over soil again with a spade.
examples: Respading of the beds improved drainage.
Spring respading is part of his gardening routine.

senses.definition: Present participle of respade: digging or turning over soil again with a spade.
examples: He is respading the garden to prepare it for planting.
Respading the soil helps roots grow more easily.

senses.definition: To extend or stretch across something again, or to arrange something so it spans a space again.
examples: Engineers will respan the cable across the river.
They decided to respan the bridge with stronger beams.

senses.definition: A playful or invented word sometimes used with the sense ‘to decorate again with small bright or shiny pieces’.
examples: She decided to respangle the costume with extra sequins.
The poster was respangled with glitter and stars.

senses.definition: To shine or sparkle again, or to make something shine again.
examples: The cleaned jewels began to resparkle under the lights.
A short break helped her eyes resparkle with energy.

senses.definition: A very rare or obsolete form meaning to pass again or pass back, mainly found in older texts.
examples: The messenger respassed the gate before nightfall.
The river respasses the boundary of the old estate.
